speaker: interviewer
text: Number two. Sigi Hart, April 19, 1996, Birkenau. So after, after you left, you left quarantine, after you left quarantine camp, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left-- I didn't leave. They, they transported us over to the [INAUDIBLE]. Transport, I mean walking over. You walked over to the [INAUDIBLE] who was here, someplace, the working camp. And we were put again into barracks and were chosen for a certain kommando. This means you were put in a certain group. You know you belong to this group. And you walk out with them every morning. And you come back every evening with them. And, and you have to be ready in the morning to walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the rules were the same. You got your time in the morning to wash in a, in a hurry, and to get your sou. And you started to, to group together in kommandos.

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends how many people were in a kommando. If you were in a small kommando, it was easier. In a bigger kommando, you have to wait til everybody's ready, because the Germans wanted you to march out from the camp in a row of five so that they can count better. Because when you walk through the gate, they don't want to have any kommando to be stopped, so because it holds back all the rest of the kommandos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the kapo made sure that when we started walking-- and we walk like soldier, of course. We had to, to go all left, right, left, right. And when we came to the gate, the kapo are shouting [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], up you grabbed your rag from the head. And you marched through like a soldier, you know, with your-- with making the noise. It was a loud noise, because everyone was wearing those wooden, wooden shoes. So we walked out with the kommando. And we walked, depends-- some kommandos were 40 minutes away. Some kommandos were 20 minutes away. And you had to walk and stay in line, like a company of soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once you get to the working place, you started working. There was one German. Oh, he was a foreman. He distributed the work, what has to be done. When it was construction, we were put in to pull the wagons with, with pipes on it and with material for building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three men had to carry a telephone post of-- oh, how tall is a telephone post, 15 meters? About 10 meters-- 10 meters, eight meter-- yeah, well, I'm converting from feet. So we were three, three men to carry a post or to push the wagon, shovel dirt, make the holes for the telephone post, and work.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really, really hard work. And there was no rest you can take. Of course, the kapo sometimes went. And they had the little shack when you went in with the German foreman. They were more on a, on a friendlier relationship. And we were working up. When we saw this, we tried to really to take a break. But we are always watching the door. In case we see the door moving, we all were working hard again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was, this was our work here in this [INAUDIBLE], and went on every day. And we had selections. We had a selection once in 10 days, once in two weeks. They called out the blocksperre. All the Jews knew already it is selection when they call blocksperre. All the Jews had to stay in the barracks while the Gentile people were going to their kommandos, to go out to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: After, we got this little piece of bread and the juice, what they called coffee. We undressed. We had to be undressed until this German commission came in. If they came at noon time, so you were for five hours naked, standing there and waiting for them. And if you were lucky, they came after an hour. So you could get dressed again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they walked in, in the barracks. They came mostly five, six SS, and the camp schreiber. The schreiber was the guy who had a big block with a pencil and paper. And he was writing down the numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to file in one line. And we were naked. We marched toward the officer, to the SS office, had to pull out our tongue, had to turn around to, to the rear, and then turn back. And then the SS made like this or like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew when he made like this, you had another chance of life. And when he made this, you knew you were condemned to death. Because in the same evening, they came. And they loaded you on a truck. And they were brought to the gas chambers. And what were the reactions of the people when they realize that they're chosen to death? How did people react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they were crying. They were screaming, because they knew their fate was sealed. There was no help. There was no getting off the list. Because the blocksperre means you cannot leave the barrack for this day until the, the group of workers came back in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so, we're, we're talking about the selection. So what were the people reactions when they realize that they're actually chosen to death?

speaker: interviewee
text: People started crying. And as we were together with Polish prisoners, of course, not, not Jewish, Gentile people who could get packages, packages from home. After two months in the camps, I learned enough to understand Polish to be like a, a translator. Because the German did not allow the Polish people to write in Polish home, send me a package. They had to write it in, in German. But then, they knew I'm not Italian. I'm a, a German. They came to me and ask if I can help them to write a letter home in German. And they will compensate me for-- they will give me some food. Because it was impossible to, to work and to live on the food you got-- the ration what you got here. And in the evenings, when, when people were going to rest, people come to the bunk with a piece of paper with a special card from the Germans what you had to use. And I wrote them. They were explaining to me, write them this and this. I am h-- I'm healthy. And I feel good. They had to write this. And please send me a package so that it helps me with my food ration. And for this, I got an extra onion, or I got a little piece of bread, or I got the little piece of salami. And those people-- now to come back-- who were selected for the gas chamber, they were raiding the, the Polish beds. They said, we're going to die tonight, at least lets us die with a full stomach. So they raided the packages. Because you cannot take the package to work. So they were under the bed pillow. And the in-- the people who were in the same bunk, they know where the package were laying. They wouldn't dare doing it if they wouldn't be condemned. Because when the poor Polish people came back and they saw the package were empty, they beat the hell out of us. Doesn't matter if you were condemned or not. Because we ate with the ones who were condemned. There were extra food, we ate it. So this is one little episode. And I took extra job on the weekend. This means weekend on Sunday, when we had-- we did not walk. We did not go out to work. But we had lauskontrolle, what takes a couple hours, at least three hours, to-- for an inspection that you have no lice. Then the hair had to be cut every two weeks. Because when it became a certain length that-- and you don't cut it-- you're under suspicion of escaping. Because with a shaved head, you cannot escape. And by doing this, I got some extra soup from the [? block ?] [? register. ?] And I also did sometimes night watch. Night watch was, we did not have a toilet in the barracks. You-- we had a big barrel. And everybody came and made into the barrel. And the night watch had to watch out that when the barrel comes three-quarter full, to stop the, the person who comes the last to, to, to make in the barrel, the two last one to keep him to take out the barrel to the toilet. And this was a big job. Because in the, in the winter time, you cannot get dressed. Because it's night. So you had to go over to your underwear and with the two sticks, carrying the barrel to the toilet. And you had to turn it over. And sometimes, you, you get wet from all the pee there. So and you, you had to wash with ice water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody who was close to the barrel from the bunk could see its three-quarter. They would rather hold on, even pee in bed, not to go out at night to the-- to do this service. And this brought me in some extra food, too, and washing some socks or pants from the kapos. They had the boots, shiny boots. We would shine the boots and did all kind of extra work to survive. And who are the kapos? What kind of people they were, were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kapos were from all, from all, all kind of people. You had Polish, French. There were Jewish. And there were non-Jewish. There were good kapos. And there were very bad kapos. I mean, it depends. We were, we were, we were under their control. If you had a good kapo, so he was beating you less. If you had a bad kapo, you can march home sometimes from work and a carry a dead person, because there was no question asked if somebody was beaten to death. And to make it a little harder on us, after work, if they were constructing-- continuing to construct in the camp, the whole kommando had to bring in two bricks under their arm. Not enough you were after 12 or 14 hours outside working, you had to take two bricks under your arm. And you had to march in with them. And then you had to put them in a certain place where the building is being done. And you can imagine if 30,000 people walk out and everybody brings four blocks in, you don't need the automa-- trucks, transportation to bring in those bricks. So that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any people attempting to escape from your kommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I know from an escape was one morning, we heard that three prisoner escaped. And they caught two. And these two were shot on the, on the, on the outside. They were brought, they brought into the camp. They were sat on a chair at the gate where we were marching out for work. They were sitting on a chair held by a stick in the back. And they made sure that we could see the ho-- the bullets holes showing us this happens when you try to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, in your kommando, you had the case of people killing themselves because of the situation they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself have not witnessed. But I've heard about that people-- I myself, I always was optimistic. I always said to myself-- by the way, my mother was born in [? Oswiecim ?]. And I always said to myself, Siggy, you're not going to die where your mother is born. You will get out of here. Though that we saw the chimney smoking and the, the fire coming out from the chimney, I always had hope. I never gave up. And this was very, very important in my, in my case to keep up the morale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever got sick, sick enough to, to seek for medical assistance here at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- matter of fact, I got sick at the time. It was just at the time when we were supposed to, to, to march out from, from the camp when the Russian came. I had tonsillitis and fever. And the rumor was that they're going ev-- evacuate the, the camp. And whoever is not able to walk will be shot in the camp. Because the crematorium in, in January of 1945, ceased to exist. They did not burn people in the end of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To come back to this camp, one day on, in April of 1944, they were forming transports. And whenever you hear transport, you were scared. Because you didn't know. Is it to go to the gas, or is it a legitimate transport? So everybody was called out by the numbers, of course. And we were about to 2,000 each-- 2,000 on one side, 2,000 on the other side. And the, the message, what was rumoring around was these two transports are going to Warsaw to clean up the Warsaw ghetto after the-- after the uprise. And in the last minute, they cancelled our transport where I was in and send us over to Monowitz camp, Auschwitz-III, what was called Buna, working for IG Farben.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we are walking here to Auschwitz, what is Birkenau, actually. Birkenau was constructed by the inmates from Auschwitz. And as you can see here, this is, this was all barracks, barracks, and barracks. And the [INAUDIBLE] was approximately over there. The quarantine lager was over there. And there's a Gypsy-- this was a woman-- the women were on this side of the camp. And a little more right to this side was the Gypsy camp. And you can see where the trees are. Approximately there and there, there were two, two-- I remember four crematoriums. And they were smoking and shooting fire out from the chimney day and night. It was ongoing day and night, especially when the Hungarian transports arrived. Everything had to be done running. If you, if you walk on, on-- if you were walking in the camp on your day when you were doing chores in camp and an SS men walked by, you had to walk by like a soldier and take off your hat. I witnessed once where one, one inmate, he thought the SS man didn't see him. He let the inmate pass. And then he turned around and called him, called him toward him. And he almost beated him to death, because he did not take his hat off. And he said, next time, you will be shot. And the [INAUDIBLE] in this camp was going up to the, the end of the, of the, where your eye can see. It looks much different. It looks like, just like a, a ghost town. And that, that's it. As you can see here, there's a few barracks left. And when you see in front of you, there's just chimneys. These places were hustling and bustling with people, with thousands of people. And now, all what's left, it's looking like a, like a cemetery, just crying to the heaven to remember, not to forget, Birkenau and Auschwitz and all the other camps. That's all what's left. There was a-- I was told there was a very cold winter here a few years ago. And people came and took apart the barracks for firewood. This is what it was worth for, just for firewood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us, what is this place where we are now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are standing here on the grounds of Buna Monowitz. We were working for IG Farben. And as you can see behind me, the big chimney was build by prisoners. And unfortunately, you cannot see much what's left, because the Russian occupation here after the war took everything. What was not cemented in the ground, they took with them. And what you see there is what's left over. We walked out from this camp every morning, marched to the, to the factory, to IG Farben, and worked there for the whole day. And at night, we came back to the camps. And here, people had opportunities to, to escape. Because I think we were about, oh, close to 100,000 people working here, including French laborers.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? INT: ?] Forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forced labor, British world, world war prisoners, and then of course, the concentration camp inmates. We could not have any contact with them. If you were caught talking to one, you could get 24 on your behind and be in the bunker for a month. So there was no communication for the regular inmates of the camp. There may have been, like Freddy before mentioned, some people who had contact and who were involved in the underground in the camp who were transmitting messages in and out from the camp this way. This place was known probably by the Allied, because a few times-- I cannot see too much-- but a few times, they send airplanes here to bombard the place. And as soon as the Germans knew that they're coming, they had in all directions, big, big barrels here where they released a kind of smoke to cover up the factory and the camp. And we couldn't go-- when they were bombarding-- we couldn't go to, to shelter. So they build for themself over there in the corner, they build shelters where, where they went. When the bombs was falling, they went to those shelters over there and were hidden while the bombs were falling. We, we didn't have shelters. So this was, this was our working place here. For, for me, it was almost for a year. And January 18, 1945, when the Russian approached, we could hear already the heavy artillery. The Germans took Auschwitz I, II, and III, and other camps. It was over 100,000 people walking in the infamous March of Death. We were walking from here to Gleiwitz. In Gleiwitz, they were already street fighting. And they had time to put us on the flat wagons-- open, not closed. It was snowing. They transported us to another camp. And from Gleiwitz, we went to Nordhausen. Nordhausen was also a big factory for the V1 and V2 rockets, where we were working three months. And when the American came, they send us over to Bergen-Belsen. And in Bergen-Belsen, we were liberated after two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, what was-- how was life here in Buna? Was it easier to be when you were in Birkenau and quarantine camp? Or it was better here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a way, in a way, we, we didn't see the ga-- the, the crematorium, the ovens smoking and shooting fire out from the chimney. In this concern, we were better off here. We knew that if we work, and we are strong enough, we could live another day, though they came also every two weeks here with selections. And the last selection I went through, they took about 90% out from the people, because they had new arrivals from Hungary in the end of the '40s. And so they did not, they did not need to people who were working for a few months here. And they're getting tired and, and skinny, so they had new, new people to come and work. But like I said before, fortunately for us, the Russian came. And we could to leave this camp here. And we-- the German, though, made sure to tell us that we should not think that we will be liberated. Because before they go, they will have time to shoot us all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said, you said that this chimney over there reminds you so many things from the, from the time that it was-- what's, what was the-- do you know what was the purpose of this very chimney? What was, was it used for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This chimney, I think, is, is a chimney from a power station. But I don't know. In, in my mind, it, it, it stayed forever. All the time, as soon as somebody mentioned Buna, this chimney came into my, my mind. And a train going by-- a working train, not a passenger train-- a train went by here and sounding the horn from the train. And certain things with people stays in. This is what stayed with me, this chimney. And this is why I wanted so much to come here and see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any particular story about, about this place, about your life in Buna, that you, that you want to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were French transports coming in. I, I lived a while in France, also hidden, during my story. And I spoke pretty good French. And when new people came in, I found a young boy my age who happened to be in the same barrack. And we became kind of friendly. And I remember his name was Simon. And he had some friends where he sometime got soup. I got sometimes some soup. And we shared. We shared so, like brothers. Unfortunately, I don't know what happened to him. Because after the march from here to Gleiwitz, we got away from each other. And I never heard from him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say as a kind of a, kind of a personal message that will be kept?

speaker: interviewee
text: A personal message-- I can only say that people shouldn't become animals. They should respect other people. And when this will be happening, we will have a much nicer world to live in. And I think this is my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name and people won't know who Freddy is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Unless it's cross-referenced to his testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to, you want me to ma--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you wanted [? shares ?] or a particular story besides Simon? Is there-- do you remember any watching any atrocities? Do you remember any good moments, bad moments, happiness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all had, we all had bad moments. I mean, it was-- we were not shortcoming of bad moments, you know. It was not a picnic camp here, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you, do you remember your emotions, your feelings, your day-to-day? Did you just-- how did, how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt lousy. We were walking out in pajamas to work, you know. And, and I mentioned it in Birkenau, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there any fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I think what you should say is that as hard as we worked, we, we stayed human.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all you want is to survive with honor. That's a good message, no? [? INT 1: ?] [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: The German wanted us to become animals. And as unhumane we were treated, we stayed human beings. We stayed human and treated people humanly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- you were talking about IG Farben. What they were doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: IG Farben was more or less an experimental factory. They're trying-- they tried to, to make gasoline from coal and also trying to make rubber for tires for their military trucks, for the tanks, for all of the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe, so maybe would like to introduce--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was together here in this camp at the time when a, a good friend of mine, Freddy Diamond. Freddy Diamond--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah!

speaker: interviewee
text: Freddy-- he came with me here from the, from the United States. We came to visit this camp and to honor, to honor our friends who are not with us anymore. And unfortunately, I was a witness of Freddy's brother who was murdered here by the Germans. He was trying to help other people. And this was his biggest crime, that he helped other people to stay human. He was hung in this camp. And the whole camp of 12,000 to 15,000 men had to stay, watch it, and march by to see what happened when somebody does something bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it has just-- it had just the opposite effect on us. It gave us at least a will to live for something. He was a hero in our eyes. What do you have to say, Freddy? Do you have any comment?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I look back at Buna. And I say clearly, because of the experiences that we had in Buna, we both of us became better human beings. We became more sensitive to other people. And we feel that we have an obligation, not only to ourselves, also to our fellow man.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is the lesson that I have learned from, from the experience in the Holocaust. And obviously, we are great supporters and fanatic supporters of the state of Israel. Because there's doubt, as long as the state of Israel, powerful and just-- there'll never be the Holocaust of Jewish people. Never, never, never-- of this, of this we are convinced, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all that I have to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
